parameters.content:
# S6 — Expressions Reference

**Read-only cheat sheet.** All 108+ n8n expressions, grouped by data type.

Open this workflow side-by-side with your real workflows. Click any Set node to see live evaluation against the sample JSON.

**How to use:**
1. Run the Manual Trigger → 'Set Example Json' to load sample data
2. Click any *_Functions_Summary node to see all expressions evaluated
3. Copy the expression you need into your own workflow

⚠️ This workflow is for reference only — not a runnable pipeline.

assignments.name: Summay
assignments.value:
# string functions 
## SUGGESTED
1) includes() 
# Meaning: .includes() → checks if a string contains a specific substring. It returns true if found, false if not. 
# Use Case in n8n: Used in IF nodes, Set nodes, or expressions when you want to check if text includes certain words, patterns, or phrases.

2) split()
# Meaning: Splits a string into an array based on a separator (like space, comma, etc.).
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you need to break text into parts — for example, splitting names, tags, or CSV-like data in a Set node or Function node.

3.1) .startsWith()
# Meaning: Checks if a string begins with a specific substring. Returns true/false.
# Use Case in n8n: Used in IF nodes or Set nodes to filter or branch data — for example, checking if an email, filename, or message starts with a certain prefix.

3.2) .endsWith()
# Meaning: Checks if a string ends with a specific substring. Returns true/false.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to detect file types, suffixes, or text endings.

4) .replaceAll()
# Meaning: Replaces all occurrences of a specific substring inside a string with another value.
# Use Case in n8n: Used in Set or Function nodes to clean or format text, remove unwanted characters, or standardize data before passing it forward.

5) .length (paramater)
# Meaning: Returns the number of items in a string or array.
  • For strings → number of characters.
  • For arrays → number of elements.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to count items or check if something is empty inside IF, Set, or Function nodes.

## EDIT
6.1) .base64Encode()
# Meaning: Converts normal text into Base64 format — a way to represent binary/text data safely (used in APIs, files, images, etc.).
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you need to encode text before sending it to another service (like an API that expects Base64).

6.2) .base64Decode()
# Meaning: Converts Base64 back to normal text.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you receive encoded data (like from a webhook or API) and need to make it readable or usable again.

7) .concat()
# Meaning: Joins (concatenates) two or more strings together into one.
# Use Case in n8n: Used in Set nodes, Function nodes, or expressions to build messages, combine variables, or construct file names, URLs, or IDs dynamically.

8) .extractDomain()
# Meaning: Extracts the domain name from a URL or email.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to identify or filter data by website or company domain.

9) .extractEmail()
# Meaning: Finds and returns an email address inside a text string.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to clean or extract emails from messages, forms, or logs.

10) .extractUrl()
# Meaning: Finds and returns the first URL inside a text string.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you receive a message or API response containing links.

11) .extractUrlPath()
# Meaning: Extracts the path part of a URL (everything after the domain).
# Use Case in n8n: Used to work with API endpoints or clean URLs for routing or analysis.

12) .hash()
# Meaning: Creates a hash value (a fixed-length encoded string) from your text — useful for encryption-like purposes or generating unique IDs. (It doesn’t decrypt — hashing is one-way.)
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you need to anonymize, verify, or uniquely identify data — like generating consistent IDs, protecting sensitive info, or checking if data changed.

13) .quote()
# Meaning: Wraps your string in double quotes (" ") — escapes any inner quotes if needed.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you need to format text for JSON, logs, or API requests, where strings must be wrapped in quotes.


14) .removeMarkdown()
# Meaning: Removes Markdown formatting from a string — like **bold**, _italic_, # headers, - lists, links, etc., leaving only plain text.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you need clean, plain text for emails, messages, databases, or APIs that don’t support Markdown.

15) .removeTags()
# Meaning: Removes HTML or XML tags from a string, leaving only plain text.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you need clean text from web pages, emails, or API responses that contain HTML.

16) .replace()
# Meaning: Replaces the first occurrence of a substring or pattern in a string with another value.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you want to modify text slightly — for example, replacing a single word, character, or placeholder before sending it to another node or API.

17) .replaceSpecialChars()
# Meaning: Replaces special characters (like !@#$%^&*()) with safe alternatives or removes them.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when preparing clean text for filenames, URLs, IDs, or any system that cannot handle special characters.

18) .slice()
# Meaning: Returns a part of a string between start and end positions.
  • start → index to begin extraction (0-based).
  • end → index to stop extraction (not included).
  • If end is omitted, extracts till the end.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you want to extract substrings like IDs, codes, prefixes, or portions of text in a Set or Function node.

19) .substring()
# Meaning: Returns a portion of a string between two indices.
  • start → index to begin extraction (0-based).
  • end → index to stop extraction (not included).
  • Swaps start and end automatically if start > end.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you need to extract specific parts of text, like IDs, codes, or sections of a string in a Set or Function node.

20) .toJsonString()
# Meaning: Converts a JavaScript object or value into a JSON-formatted string.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you need to send data as JSON in HTTP requests, API calls, or logs, or when storing structured data as a single string.



21) .trim()
# Meaning: Removes whitespace from the start and end of a string.
  • Does not remove spaces in the middle.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you need clean, trimmed text before saving, comparing, or sending data in Set, Function, or IF nodes.

22.1) .trimStart()
# Meaning: Removes whitespace only from the start of a string.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you want to clean leading spaces but keep trailing spaces intact.

22.2) .trimEnd()
# Meaning: Removes whitespace only from the end of a string.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you want to clean trailing spaces but keep leading spaces intact.

23.1) .urlEncode()
# Meaning: Converts a string into a URL-safe format, replacing spaces and special characters with % encoding.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when sending data in query parameters or HTTP requests where URLs must be properly encoded.

23.2) .urlDecode()
# Meaning: Converts a URL-encoded string back into normal text.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when receiving URL-encoded data from webhooks, APIs, or query parameters to make it readable or processable.

24) .indexOf()
# Meaning: Returns the position (index) of the first occurrence of a substring in a string.
  • Returns -1 if the substring is not found.
  • Indexing starts at 0.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to check if a substring exists and find its position in a string — useful in IF nodes, Function nodes, or when manipulating text.

25) .lastIndexOf()
# Meaning: Returns the position (index) of the last occurrence of a substring in a string.
  • Returns -1 if the substring is not found.
  • Indexing starts at 0.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you want to find the last appearance of a word or character — useful in IF nodes, Function nodes, or text manipulation before slicing or extracting.

26) .match() (https://regex101.com/)
# Meaning: Searches a string for a pattern (usually a regular expression) and returns matches.
  • Returns an array of matches or null if none found.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you need to extract specific patterns from text — like emails, phone numbers, codes, or any regex-based data — inside Function or Set nodes.

27) .search()
# Meaning: Searches a string for a pattern (string or regex) and returns the index of the first match.
  • Returns -1 if the pattern is not found.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you want to find the position of a keyword or pattern in a string, often for IF node conditions or text processing.

28) .isDomain()
# Meaning: Checks if a string is a valid domain name (like example.com).
  • Returns true if it is a valid domain, false otherwise.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you want to validate or filter domain names from URLs, emails, or user input — often in IF nodes or Function nodes.

29) .isUrl()
# Meaning: Checks if a string is a valid URL. Returns true/false.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to validate URLs from webhooks, forms, or API responses before processing or routing in workflow nodes.

30) .isEmail()
# Meaning: Checks if a string is a valid email address. Returns true/false.
# Use Case in n8n: Validate email inputs from forms, webhooks, or APIs before processing.

31) .isEmpty()
# Meaning: Checks if a string is empty ("") or only contains whitespace.
# Use Case in n8n: Validate if a field has no content before sending, saving, or branching logic.

32) .isNotEmpty()
# Meaning: Checks if a string has content (opposite of .isEmpty()).
# Use Case in n8n: Validate that a field contains meaningful data before proceeding.

33) .isNumeric()
# Meaning: Checks if a string represents a number (integer or decimal).
# Use Case in n8n: Used to validate numeric input before calculations, database insertion, or API calls.

34) .toLowerCase()
# Meaning: Converts all letters in a string to lowercase.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to normalize text for comparisons, searches, or consistent formatting in Set, Function, or IF nodes.

35) .toUpperCase()
# Meaning: Converts all letters in a string to uppercase.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to normalize text or emphasize values for consistent formatting in Set, Function, or IF nodes.

36) .toSentenceCase()
# Meaning: Converts a string so that the first letter of the sentence is uppercase and the rest are lowercase.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to format text neatly for messages, emails, or database entries in Set or Function nodes.

37) .toSnakeCase()
# Meaning: Converts a string to snake_case — lowercase words separated by underscores (_).
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you need consistent naming for variables, filenames, database fields, or API parameters.

38) .toTitleCase()
# Meaning: Converts a string so that the first letter of each word is uppercase and the rest are lowercase.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to format names, titles, or headings in Set or Function nodes for better readability and presentation.

39) .parseJson()
# Meaning: Converts a JSON-formatted string into a JavaScript object.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when receiving JSON as a string (from APIs, webhooks, or text fields) and you need to access its properties.

40) .toBoolean()
# Meaning: Converts a string like "true"/"false" or numbers "0"/"1" into a Boolean value.
# Use Case in n8n: Useful for IF nodes or logic operations where the input is a string but needs to be treated as true/false.

41) .toDateTime()
# Meaning: Converts a string into a Date object.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when parsing dates from text, webhooks, or API responses for comparisons, filtering, or formatting.

42) .toNumber()
# Meaning: Converts a string into a numeric value (integer or decimal).
# Use Case in n8n: Useful for calculations, comparisons, or numeric validation when input comes as a string.

assignments.name: match_Ex
assignments.value: ={{"hay man, how are you doing today?".match(/hay/)}}

assignments.name: Summay
assignments.value:
1) .round()
# Meaning: Rounds a number to the nearest integer.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you need whole numbers for calculations, display, or IDs in Set or Function nodes.

2) .floor()
# Meaning: Rounds a number down to the nearest integer.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you want to always drop decimals (e.g., inventory, pricing, or indexing).

3) .ceil()
# Meaning: Rounds a number up to the nearest integer.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you want to always round up (e.g., shipping costs, quotas, or limits).

4) .abs()
# Meaning: Returns the absolute value of a number (removes the negative sign).
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you need only positive numbers for calculations, differences, or comparisons in Set or Function nodes.

5) .format()
# Meaning: Formats a number into a string with specified formatting, like decimal places, commas, or currency symbols.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you want to display numbers in a readable or standardized way in emails, messages, or reports.

6.1) .isOdd()
# Meaning: Checks if a number is odd. Returns true or false.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you want to branch workflow logic based on odd numbers (e.g., alternating actions, validations).

6.2) .isEven()
# Meaning: Checks if a number is even. Returns true or false.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you want to branch workflow logic based on even numbers.

7) .isInteger()
Meaning: Checks if a number is a whole number (no decimals). Returns true or false.
Use Case in n8n: Used to validate numeric input before processing, storing in databases, or performing calculations that require integers.

8) .toBoolean()
# Meaning: Converts a number into a Boolean:
  • 0 → false
  • Any other number → true
# Use Case in n8n: Used for conditional logic when numbers determine workflow decisions.

9) .toDateTime() (https://www.unixtimestamp.com/) add 000 after the time stamp
# Meaning: Converts a number (timestamp) into a Date object.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when handling timestamps from APIs or databases and converting them for filtering, formatting, or comparisons.

10) .toLocaleString()
# Meaning: Formats a number into a string with locale-specific formatting (commas, decimals, etc.).
# Use Case in n8n: Used when displaying numbers readably in messages, emails, reports, or dashboards.

11) .toString()
# Meaning: Converts a number into a string.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you need to combine numbers with text, send them to APIs, or store as string values.

assignments.name: Summary
assignments.value:
1) .length
# Meaning:Returns the number of elements in the array.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to check array size before looping, filtering, or making decisions in IF or Function nodes.

2.1) .first() 
# Meaning: Returns the first element of an array.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you want the earliest item or first value in a list, often for processing or routing in Set, Function, or IF nodes.

2.2) .last()
# Meaning: Returns the last element of the array.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you want the most recent item or last value in a list.

3) .includes()
# Meaning: Checks if the array contains a specific value. Returns true/false.
# Use Case in n8n:Used to validate or filter data before proceeding in a workflow.

4) .map(item => item)
# Meaning: Creates a new array by transforming each element using a function.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to modify array elements before passing to other nodes.

5) .filter(item => true)
# Meaning: Creates a new array with elements that match a condition.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to remove unwanted items or keep only certain values in a workflow.

6) .append(value) arrays can take multiple data types.
# Meaning: Adds one or more elements to the end of an array.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to dynamically add items to an array during workflow execution, for example when collecting results from multiple nodes before sending or processing them.

7) .chunk(size)
# Meaning: Splits an array into **smaller arrays** (chunks) of a specified size.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you need to **process or send array data in batches**, for example sending API requests in groups or handling large datasets step by step.

8) .compact()
# Meaning: Removes **falsy values** (`null`, `undefined`, `false`, `0`, `""`, `NaN`) from an array.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to **clean arrays** before processing, ensuring that only meaningful values are passed to other nodes.

9) .concat(array2)
# Meaning: Combines the **original array** with one or more arrays or values into a **new array**.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to **merge multiple arrays** or add extra elements before sending data to other nodes or APIs.

10) .difference(array2)
# Meaning: Returns a new array containing elements from the **original array** that are **not present** in `array2`.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to **filter out unwanted or duplicate items** when comparing arrays, for example removing already processed IDs or tasks.

11) .find(item => true)
# Meaning: Returns the **first element** in the array that **matches a condition** defined in the callback function.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to **locate a specific item** in an array based on a condition, often for routing or extracting data in Function or IF nodes.

12) .indexOf(value)
# Meaning: Returns the **index (position)** of the **first occurrence** of a value in an array. Returns `-1` if the value is not found.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to **check the position of an element** in an array or to determine if it exists before performing actions in IF or Function nodes.

13) .intersection(array2)
# Meaning: Returns a new array containing elements that are present in both arrays.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to find common items between arrays, for example matching IDs, tags, or values from multiple sources.

14.1) .isEmpty()
# Meaning: Checks if an array **has no elements**. Returns `true` if empty, `false` otherwise.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to **validate or branch workflow logic** based on whether an array contains any data.

14.2) .isNotEmpty()
# Meaning: Checks if an array **contains one or more elements**. Returns `true` if not empty, `false` otherwise.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to **ensure there is data to process** before performing actions in Function or IF nodes.

15) .join(separator)
# Meaning: Converts an array into a **string**, joining elements with the specified separator (default is `,`).
# Use Case in n8n: Used to **create a string from array elements**, for example sending CSV data or concatenating values for messages.


16) .merge(array2)
# Meaning: Combines the **original array** with another array into a **single array**.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to **combine multiple arrays** before processing or sending to another node.

17) .pluck(key)
# Meaning: Extracts a specific **property value** from each object in an array.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to **get a list of specific fields** from an array of objects.

18) .randomItem()
# Meaning: Returns a **random element** from the array.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to **pick random items** for testing, sampling, or dynamic workflows.

19) .reduce(item => item)
# Meaning: Reduces the array to a **single value** using a function.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to **aggregate array elements**, like summing numbers or concatenating strings.

20) .renameKeys(mapping)
# Meaning: Renames keys in an array of objects according to a **mapping object**.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to **standardize or transform object keys** before sending to APIs or databases.

21) .reverse()
# Meaning: Reverses the **order of elements** in the array.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to **process items from last to first** or reorder arrays for display.

22) .slice(start, end)
# Meaning: Returns a **portion of the array** from `start` to `end` (exclusive).
# Use Case in n8n: Used to **extract specific segments** of an array for processing.

23) .smartJoin(separator)
# Meaning: Joins array elements into a **readable string** with smart separators (like commas and “and”).
# Use Case in n8n: Used to **format array elements** nicely for messages or notifications.

24) .sort(compareFn)
# Meaning: Sorts the array **based on a comparison function**.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to **order array elements** before processing or output.

25) .toJsonString()
# Meaning: Converts the array into a **JSON string**.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when sending arrays to APIs or storing in text fields as JSON.

26) .toSpliced(start, deleteCount, ...items)
# Meaning: Creates a **new array** with elements **added/removed** at a specific position (does not mutate original).
# Use Case in n8n: Used to **insert or remove items** from an array without changing the original data.

27) .union(array2)
# Meaning: Returns a new array containing **all unique elements** from both arrays.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to **merge arrays** while avoiding duplicates.

28) .unique()
# Meaning: Returns a new array containing **only unique elements** (removes duplicates).
# Use Case in n8n: Used to **clean arrays** and ensure no repeated values before processing.

assignments.name: Summary
assignments.value:
1) .keys()
# Meaning: Returns an array of all the keys (property names) in an object.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you need to iterate, inspect, or manipulate object keys inside Function or Set nodes.

2) .values()
# Meaning: Returns an array of all the values in an object.
# Use Case in n8n: Useful for looping through values or performing transformations on data extracted from JSON.

3) .isEmpty()
# Meaning: Checks if the object has no keys (empty). Returns true or false.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to validate if a JSON object contains data before processing or branching workflows.

4) .hasField()
# Meaning: Checks if an object contains a specific field or property.
# Use Case in n8n: Often used in IF or Switch nodes to verify if expected data fields exist before further actions.

5) .compact()
# Meaning: Removes any fields with null, undefined, or empty values from the object.
# Use Case in n8n: Helps clean data before storing, exporting, or processing in integrations.

6) .isNotEmpty()
# Meaning: Checks if an object contains at least one field. Opposite of .isEmpty().
# Use Case in n8n: Used to ensure a node’s output has valid data before continuing workflow execution.

7) .keepFieldsContaining()
# Meaning: Keeps only the fields in an object that match a specific substring or pattern.
# Use Case in n8n: Useful when you want to retain only relevant data (e.g., fields containing “email” or “id”).

8) .removeField()
# Meaning: Removes a specific field from an object by key name.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to clean sensitive or unnecessary data before sharing with other systems.

9) .removeFieldsContaining()
# Meaning: Removes all fields whose names contain a specific substring.
# Use Case in n8n: Helps sanitize or simplify data structures by removing unwanted fields.

10) .toJsonString()
# Meaning: Converts an object into a JSON-formatted string.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when sending data to APIs, storing in text fields, or logging structured data.

11) .urlEncode()
# Meaning: Encodes the object into a URL-safe format (query string style).
# Use Case in n8n: Used when preparing data for HTTP requests or building dynamic URLs with parameters.

parameters.content:
# Expressions (explained)

This table shows common n8n expression patterns (the content in the image). n8n evaluates any expression wrapped in double curly braces `{{ ... }}`. Inside those braces you can read data from the current item, other nodes, call helper functions, do date formatting, run conditions, and more.

## Quick syntax
- Wrap any expression in `{{ ... }}`.
- Use `$json` to access the current item's JSON.
- Use `$node["NodeName"].json` to access another node's output.
- Use `$now` for the current date/time and format it with `.format(...)`.
- Use JavaScript expressions (comparisons, ternary operator, arithmetic) inside the braces.

---

## Row-by-row explanation

- Basic
  - Example: `{{ $json["key"] }}`
  - What it does: returns the value of the property `key` from the current item’s JSON.

- Node notation (accessing other node output)
  - Example: `{{ $node["Other Node"].json["value"] }}`
  - What it does: reads the field `value` from the output of the node named `Other Node`. Use the exact node name.

- Date formatting
  - Example: `{{ $now.format("YYYY-MM-DD") }}`
  - What it does: formats the current date/time. Use the supported format tokens (e.g., `YYYY`, `MM`, `DD`, `HH`, `mm`, `ss`).

- Condition
  - Example: `{{ $json["price"] > 100 }}`
  - What it does: evaluates to `true` or `false`. Useful in "If" nodes or to decide a value.

- Ternary operator
  - Example: `{{ $json["price"] > 100 ? "expensive" : "cheap" }}`
  - What it does: returns `"expensive"` when the condition is true, otherwise `"cheap"`.

- Built-in methods
  - Examples: `$jmespath(...)`, `$fromAll(...)`, `$max(...)`, `$min(...)`, ...
  - What they do: helper functions provided by n8n for common transformations and queries. See the n8n docs for full list and usage:
    - https://docs.n8n.io/code/builtin/data-transformation-functions/

- Metadata / workflow variables
  - Common names you can access inside expressions:
    - `$execution` — execution-level data
    - `$itemIndex` (or similar item index variable) — index of the current item
    - `$input` — raw input data
    - `$parameters` — node parameters (the values configured on the node)
    - `$prevNode` / `$node` — access other nodes
    - `$runIndex` / `$runIndex`-style — run counters
    - `$today` / `$now` — date/time helpers
    - `$vars` — workflow variables
    - `$workflow` — workflow metadata
    - `$nodeVersion` — node version info
  - What they do: let you access execution, environment and workflow-related metadata inside expressions.

---

## Tips / best practices
- Keep expressions small and test them in the node's UI (Expression editor) to confirm results.
- When accessing another node, use the exact node name and the `.json[...]` path.
- Use the ternary operator for simple conditional value selection.
- For complex transformations, prefer built-in functions (`$jmespath`, `$fromAll`, etc.) or write a Function/Function Item node (JavaScript).
- Refer to the official docs for the exact available helpers and up-to-date format tokens:
  - https://docs.n8n.io/code/builtin/data-transformation-functions/

assignments.name: Summary
assignments.value:
1) .toNumber()
# Meaning: Converts a boolean value to a number — `true` becomes `1`, and `false` becomes `0`.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when performing mathematical operations or storing boolean data as numeric values (e.g., for APIs or databases).

2) .toString()
# Meaning: Converts a boolean value to its string representation — `"true"` or `"false"`.
# Use Case in n8n: Useful when preparing boolean values for text fields, logs, or when concatenating with other strings.

assignments.name: Summary
assignments.value:
1) .toDateTime().format('yyyy-MM-dd') (https://moment.github.io/luxon/#/formatting?id=table-of-tokens)
# Meaning:
  • .toDateTime() → Converts a string into a DateTime object that n8n can understand.
  • .format('yyyy-MM-dd') → Formats that date into the specified pattern (e.g., 2025-10-08).
# Use Case in n8n: Used to standardize date formats when handling timestamps from APIs or inputs — especially useful before comparing, filtering, or storing dates.

2) .minus(4, 'hour')
# Meaning: Subtracts 4 hours from the current date and time.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to adjust timestamps backward — for example, to convert from UTC to a local timezone or check records from the past few hours.

3) .plus(20, 'seconds')
# Meaning: Adds 20 seconds to the current date and time.
# Use Case in n8n: Used to schedule or offset timestamps forward, such as setting a short delay for time-based triggers or future actions.

4) .diffTo("10/8/2025".toDateTime(), 'days')
# Meaning: Calculates the difference between two dates — in this case, between the current date and `"10/8/2025"` — measured in days.
# Use Case in n8n: Used when you need to measure time gaps between two dates (e.g., to find how many days until a deadline, or how long ago something happened).

5) .extract('hours')
# Meaning: Extracts a specific part of a DateTime — here, it pulls out the hour from the current time.
# Use Case in n8n:
Used when you need to get a specific time unit (like hour, minute, or day) for logic, scheduling, or conditions — for example, triggering an action only during certain hours.

6) .startOf('month')
# Meaning: Returns a DateTime representing the first moment of the specified unit, here the start of the month (i.e., day 1 at 00:00:00).
# Use Case in n8n: Used to align dates to a specific boundary, such as the beginning of a day, week, or month — helpful for reports, billing cycles, or filtering records by month.

7) .endOf('month')
# Meaning: Returns a DateTime representing the last moment of the specified unit, here the end of the current month (i.e., the last day at 23:59:59).
# Use Case in n8n:Used to calculate or filter data within monthly periods, such as generating monthly reports, setting billing cutoffs, or comparing dates to the month’s end.
